entries.title: Always Almost There
entries.rawOfficialText:
“Science has not yet mastered prophecy,” Neil Armstrong (the first man on the moon) once said. “We predict too much for the next year and yet far too little for the next ten.” Indeed, some technologies exist in a tantalizing but perpetual “almost there”, always just a few years away. Consider the examples below, then discuss with your team: why are these technologies stuck in the near future, and which one would you be most excited to see in action?

fusion power | cure for cancer | graphene | flying cars | virtual reality
nanotechnology | space elevator | food pills | artificial general intelligence

links.label: BBC Science Focus - AI bubble and AGI limits

entries.studentExplanation:
Some technologies become permanent residents of the near future: famous enough to feel inevitable, but difficult enough to remain unfinished, niche, expensive, or socially awkward. Fusion power, flying cars, graphene, nanotechnology, life-extension dreams, and AGI all show the same pattern: the idea can be scientifically meaningful while the path to everyday use is delayed by engineering, cost, safety, regulation, scale, or exaggerated expectations. The Neil Armstrong quote matters because it warns students that humans often overestimate the immediate future and underestimate slower, deeper change.

The added futurism notes group these examples as promises that are technically imaginable but socially or physically hard: fusion has to produce usable net energy at scale, cancer is many diseases, graphene is difficult to commercialize, and space elevators require materials and orbital engineering beyond ordinary construction. Use this to audit promises: what future is being predicted, when people expected it, how technical or social limits delayed it, and why “soon” can be a feeling rather than a calendar date.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this helps students separate technical possibility from real-world arrival. At the section level, it opens futurism as a study of delayed promises, not just cool inventions. At the year-theme level, it connects to prediction and progress because being “almost there” can become its own long liminal state.
entries.takeaway: Imagination can move faster than engineering, markets, and social trust
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating whether delayed technologies are failures, long-term research bets, or examples of futurist overconfidence.
entries.counterargument: Some technologies that seem permanently delayed are still progressing; repeated over-promising does not make the underlying research useless.

items.title: Cure for cancer
items.note: The phrase “a cure for cancer” hides the fact that cancer is many diseases with different causes, mutations, and treatments.
items.imageGuidance: Use /imagesearch first to find a realistic medical or educational image showing diverse cancer cells or a cancer research lab, not a scary generic tumor stock image. The image must be realistic, not anime, and must feel visually compatible with the beige/brown/maroon WSC app UI; WSC app mandatory. If /imagesearch cannot find a perfect image and the slide is illustrating a general idea rather than an exact known source, use /imagecreator or /imagegen to create a realistic educational image that illustrates the concept while respecting the World Scholar app theme.
items.videoGuidance: Use /youtubesearch to find a clear educational video, ideally 5-7 minutes maximum or shorter, that explains why there is not one single cure for cancer. Avoid hype clips, product ads, sensational thumbnails, or videos that only decorate the topic.

items.title: Graphene
items.note: Graphene has remarkable properties, but the challenge is turning a laboratory wonder-material into cheap, reliable everyday products.
items.imageGuidance: Use /imagesearch first to find a realistic microscope/atomic lattice image or official graphene visualization from a university/Graphene Flagship-style source. The image must be realistic, not anime, and must feel visually compatible with the beige/brown/maroon WSC app UI; WSC app mandatory. Because this is a named real source/object/work, use the exact image or a reliable source version; do not replace it with a generic illustration. Do not use /imagecreator or /imagegen unless the app needs a separate supplementary conceptual background, and never as a substitute for the exact source image.
items.videoGuidance: Use /youtubesearch to find a clear educational video, ideally 5-7 minutes maximum or shorter, that explains what graphene is and why adoption has been slower than early hype. Avoid hype clips, product ads, sensational thumbnails, or videos that only decorate the topic.

items.title: Flying cars / advanced air mobility
items.note: The old dream of flying cars now looks more like eVTOL air taxis, but safety, noise, regulation, cost, and city infrastructure remain barriers.

The image must be realistic, not anime, and must feel visually compatible with the beige/brown/maroon WSC app UI; WSC app mandatory. Because this is a named real source/object/work, use the exact image or a reliable source version; do not replace it with a generic illustration. Do not use /imagecreator or /imagegen unless the app needs a separate supplementary conceptual background, and never as a substitute for the exact source image.
items.videoGuidance: Use /youtubesearch to find a clear educational video, ideally 5-7 minutes maximum or shorter, that explains advanced air mobility, eVTOLs, and why flying cars are hard to make normal. Avoid hype clips, product ads, sensational thumbnails, or videos that only decorate the topic.

items.title: Virtual reality
items.note: VR arrived, but not as a universal replacement for screens or daily life; adoption depends on comfort, cost, social use, and content.

entries.title: What 2018 Thought 2028 Would Look Like
entries.rawOfficialText: With apologies to Mr. Armstrong, sometimes people also predict too much for the next ten. Consider these predictions for the year 2028, made in 2018. Are there any that haven’t come true yet but that you think still might in the next two years?

links.label: Kindred Capital - Take me to the Year 2028

entries.studentExplanation:
A 2018 forecast for 2028 is useful not because it gives a perfect map of the future, but because it reveals what investors and technology thinkers thought was accelerating at that moment. The article predicts a new mobile form factor, major autonomous-car adoption, a huge freelance workforce, longer lives, security drones, mainstream vegetarianism, cashless payment, solar growth, enhanced athletes, VR addiction, and more Brexit talks. Students should treat the article as a time capsule: some predictions were directionally smart, some were too fast, and some reveal the hopes and fears of 2018 more than the reality of 2028.

Treat the 2018 forecast as a time capsule, not just a checklist. It predicted new phone forms, autonomous miles, freelancers, cashless payments, VR addiction, wireless charging, and altered sports; the useful exercise is to ask which predictions failed because the tech was hard, and which failed because people did not adopt them. Use this to audit promises: what future is being predicted, when people expected it, how technical or social limits delayed it, and why “soon” can be a feeling rather than a calendar date.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this teaches students to evaluate predictions after time has passed. At the section level, it gives a concrete forecast to test rather than abstract futurism. At the year-theme level, it connects to prediction and progress because the future is partly imagined from the anxieties, investments, and blind spots of the present.
entries.takeaway: Predictions are also documents of the present that produced them
examples: A forecast as a time capsule
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating whether forecasts should be judged by accuracy alone or also as evidence of the anxieties and assumptions of their moment.
entries.counterargument: Even a forecast that misses its timeline may still identify a real trend or pressure before it becomes obvious.

entries.title: Tesla’s Tower and the Meaning of “Soon”
entries.rawOfficialText: “It will soon be possible to transmit wireless messages all over the world so simply that any individual can own and operate his own apparatus,” the inventor Nikola Tesla told the New York Times in 1904. Whether it was an accurate prediction of the smartphone depends on what you think the meaning of “soon” is. For Tesla, soon meant, “I’m building it right now; I just need more funding.” Read more about his Wardenclyffe Tower and the reasons it failed, then discuss with your team: what if it had succeeded?

links.label: Tesla Science Center - History of Wardenclyffe

entries.studentExplanation:
Tesla’s 1904 prediction of personal wireless communication sounds astonishingly close to the smartphone, but Wardenclyffe shows why being visionary is not the same as arriving. Tesla imagined global wireless communication and power, and the tower was meant to be a prototype for a worldwide system. The project failed because of funding, timing, technical ambition, and lack of institutional support. Students should see Tesla as both impressively forward-looking and overconfident about how soon a world-scale system could be built.

Wardenclyffe makes “soon” historically concrete: Tesla imagined wireless communication and power before infrastructure, funding, regulation, and physics could make the dream practical. The tower failed not only as an invention but as a promise that depended on a whole system arriving with it. Use this to audit promises: what future is being predicted, when people expected it, how technical or social limits delayed it, and why “soon” can be a feeling rather than a calendar date.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this shows that a prediction can point in the right direction while still failing in its actual form. At the section level, it develops futurism as a problem of timing, funding, infrastructure, and credibility. At the year-theme level, it connects to progress and planning because the future belongs not only to big ideas, but to ideas that can survive implementation.
entries.takeaway: A prediction can be visionary and still fail as a practical route
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating whether visionary ideas matter without the funding, infrastructure, and timing needed to make them real.
entries.counterargument: Tesla's vision was striking, but treating Wardenclyffe as a direct smartphone prediction can simplify the messy history of communications infrastructure.

visualSections.title: Tesla’s Tower and the Meaning of “Soon”

entries.title: Future Promises and the Problem of Timing
entries.rawOfficialText:
Tesla is far from the only person to have declared that “it will soon be possible…” Sentences that begin with this and similar phrases (e.g., “In a few years, everyone will…”) almost always make ambitious predictions about the near future. Search online for examples like them, then discuss with your team: for each one, are we there yet, and how long did it take us to get there? If not, how close are we? Here are three examples to get you started.

“…for one man to be heard by every human being on Earth” (1925)
“…to launch a satellite that makes its own solar array in orbit” (2019)
“…for humans to live a thousand years or more” (2025)

links.label: Our World in Data - Technology adoption
Pew Research Center - Internet and technology reports

entries.studentExplanation:
Futurist language often sounds precise while remaining vague. Phrases like “soon,” “in a few years,” and “everyone will” turn uncertainty into confidence. Sometimes the prediction is not completely wrong: the technology arrives later, smaller, more unevenly, or in a form people did not expect. This entry trains students to read prediction language carefully and ask what exactly is being promised: technical possibility, mass adoption, cheap access, legal permission, or cultural acceptance.

Use this as a forecast-audit tool. Students should ask what would have to exist for a promise to arrive: materials, cost, laws, public trust, maintenance, energy, training, and demand. A technology can be invented and still not be socially ready. Use this to audit promises: what future is being predicted, when people expected it, how technical or social limits delayed it, and why “soon” can be a feeling rather than a calendar date.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, students learn that prediction is partly language analysis. At the section level, this moves futurism beyond gadgets and into rhetoric. At the year-theme level, it connects to progress because “Are we there yet?” often depends on whose timeline is being sold.
entries.takeaway: Prediction requires evidence about systems, not only ideas
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating whether confident future language motivates progress or hides uncertainty behind an illusion of inevitability.
entries.counterargument: Bold language can mislead, but it can also attract funding, attention, and effort to fragile early-stage ideas.

visualSections.title: Future Promises and the Problem of Timing

entries.title: How Long Should Humans Try to Live?
entries.rawOfficialText:
Two humans that want to live a thousand years are the presidents of China and Russia. In September 2025, they were overheard talking about using organ transplants to extend their lives indefinitely. As a strategy, organ transplants have major shortcomings: it can be hard to find the organs, and just as hard to stop our immune systems from rejecting them. But people have invested billions of dollars in developing other approaches. Learn more about those listed below, then discuss with your team: if science does find ways for us to live much longer lives, would there be any reason to hesitate before using them?

organoids | bioprinting | plasma transfusion | senotherapeutics
cellular reprogramming | cellular rejuvenation | telomere extension
caloric restriction | digital immortality | cryonics

links.label: Reuters - hot mic discussion of organ transplants and immortality
Washington Post - wager on living to 150
National Institute on Aging - aging biology

entries.studentExplanation:
Life-extension futurism mixes serious biology with extreme fantasy. The overheard Putin-Xi conversation about organ transplants and living to 150 makes the topic political as well as scientific: longer life is not just a medical dream, but also a question of power, inequality, and who gets access. Some technologies aim to repair tissues, slow cell damage, or replace organs. Others, like cryonics or digital immortality, ask whether preserving a body or data would really preserve a person. The strongest question is not only “Can humans live longer?” but “What kind of world would be created if only some people could?”

The sources make longevity a stack of partial tools: organ transplants require rejection management, edited pig organs and stem cells are experimental, organoids model organs rather than replace full bodies, and bioprinting still struggles to create working tissues at scale. The ethical question is who gets more life, not just whether more life is possible. Use this to audit promises: what future is being predicted, when people expected it, how technical or social limits delayed it, and why “soon” can be a feeling rather than a calendar date.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this helps students see life extension as science plus ethics. At the section level, it turns futurism inward toward the body and identity. At the year-theme level, it connects to bright/dark futures because longer life can sound hopeful, unfair, frightening, or politically dangerous depending on who benefits.
entries.takeaway: Whether longer life would be safe, fair, meaningful, and desirable
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating whether extreme life extension should be pursued as medical progress, political power, personal freedom, or social risk.
entries.counterargument: Longer life can sound unequal or frightening, but many life-extension tools may first appear as ordinary disease prevention or healthier aging.

visualSections.title: How Long Should Humans Try to Live? - Group 1

links.label: Reuters - hot mic discussion of organ transplants and immortality

items.title: Organoids
items.note: Organoids are mini tissue models grown in labs, useful for research but not the same as growing replacement humans or complete organs.

entries.title: Yesterday’s Tomorrow
entries.rawOfficialText: Read about some of the futuristic technologies predicted in the past, then watch this video that shows how those predictions evolved across the 20th century. Some of them seem fantastical—an Olympics on the moon in the year 2020?—but others may already be within our reach, or just slightly beyond (where they may or may not get stuck). With your team, consider: what can we learn about the past from where people thought we were going? Are any of the things that people predicted about the 21st century things you might now predict for the 22nd?

links.label: Rare Historical Photos - retro future predictions

entries.studentExplanation:
Old visions of the future are not just funny mistakes. They show what past societies thought progress should look like: faster travel, easier homes, space colonies, automated work, new foods, and dazzling machines. Some predictions seem absurd now, but others look like early versions of technologies that arrived differently. Students should read retrofuturism as a mirror: every future picture also reveals the past that imagined it.

Retrofuturism should be explained as looking at old futures from the present. Moon Olympics, automatic homes, nuclear cars, food pills, and streamlined cities reveal what past societies feared, desired, or misunderstood, so failed predictions still teach history. Use this to audit promises: what future is being predicted, when people expected it, how technical or social limits delayed it, and why “soon” can be a feeling rather than a calendar date.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this teaches students to compare accuracy with cultural meaning. At the section level, it gives futurism historical depth. At the year-theme level, it connects to prediction because “tomorrow” always carries the values, fears, and design tastes of “yesterday.”
entries.takeaway: Every imagined tomorrow also reveals the culture that imagined it
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating whether old predictions should be laughed at as failed forecasts or studied as cultural evidence.
entries.counterargument: Some retrofutures are spectacle, but spectacle still reveals what people wanted, feared, and thought progress should look like.

channelVideos.title: Past Predictions of the Future Every Decade

entries.title: Quantum Supremacy and the Fear of Q-Day
entries.rawOfficialText: Who wouldn’t want quantum supremacy? In 2019, Google made a splashy headline: they’d achieved it! Others soon claimed they had too. But researchers admit quantum computers still have no practical purpose, even as cryptographers worry that they could someday be used to hack even the most encrypted passwords. Read more about this much-dreaded Q-Day, then discuss with your team: should we be developing technologies that have unclear practical advantages but clear downsides?

links.label: CNBC - quantum computing applications and limits

entries.studentExplanation:
Quantum computing is a good example of a technology that can be impressive before it is broadly useful. “Quantum supremacy” means a quantum system performed a specific task beyond what a classical computer could easily do, but that does not mean quantum computers are ready for everyday work. At the same time, cybersecurity experts worry about Q-Day: the moment when a powerful enough quantum computer could break major encryption systems. The same technology can therefore look like a scientific triumph and a security threat before most people can use it at all.

Add the distinction between milestone and usefulness: quantum supremacy proves a quantum machine can outperform classical machines on a specific task, but it does not mean everyday quantum laptops have arrived. Q-Day is a security fear about cryptography, planning, and migration before the threat is practical. Use this to audit promises: what future is being predicted, when people expected it, how technical or social limits delayed it, and why “soon” can be a feeling rather than a calendar date.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this helps students separate breakthrough headlines from practical usefulness. At the section level, it makes futurism darker by showing that risk can arrive before benefit. At the year-theme level, it connects to bright/dark futures and prediction because the future may be powerful before it is safe.
entries.takeaway: Society must prepare for risks before everyday benefits are obvious
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating whether technologies with unclear practical benefits but plausible risks should be developed quickly, slowly, or differently.
entries.counterargument: Many valuable technologies first looked abstract or risky, so rejecting research too early can also carry social costs.

entries.title: Can We Still Tell Human Writing from AI Writing?
entries.rawOfficialText: Certainly, here are some examples of text generated by large language models. It’s not just the use of em dashes, but the use of negative parallel structures like this. Wikipedia has compiled a list of signs that a piece of text has been generated by AI. We may soon get to a point where we won’t be able to accurately distinguish human- and AI-generated content—at least, not without setting deliberate traps. Will it matter?

links.label: HuffPost / article on AI cheating concerns

entries.studentExplanation:
As AI writing becomes smoother, students should move beyond simple “spot the em dash” detection. Wikipedia’s list of signs matters because it shows that AI text often has patterns: generic balance, over-explanation, polished emptiness, formulaic contrast, or repeated structures. But the deeper issue is authorship. If a text sounds human enough, readers still need to ask who is responsible for its claims, whether the writer understands them, and whether school assignments are measuring thinking or just finished output.

The explanation should move beyond “AI sounds generic.” Students should ask who is responsible for a text, whether detectors are reliable, how style can be imitated, and why assessment changes when writing can be produced quickly but not always truthfully or thoughtfully. Use this to audit promises: what future is being predicted, when people expected it, how technical or social limits delayed it, and why “soon” can be a feeling rather than a calendar date.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this helps students think about trust and authorship, not only cheating. At the section level, it brings futurism into school and writing. At the year-theme level, it connects to drafts and bright/dark futures because AI can help produce text while also making source, voice, and responsibility harder to see.
entries.takeaway: Whether source and authorship still matter when output looks convincing
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating whether writing should be judged only by output quality or also by authorship, responsibility, and declared process.
entries.counterargument: Detection may become harder, but context, disclosure, assessment design, and accountability can still make authorship meaningful.

entries.title: What Would AGI Change?
entries.rawOfficialText: Deep learning-driven neural networks excel at competing high school English assignments, but it’s not clear that they’re getting anywhere close to artificial general intelligence (AGI)—that is, to an AI that mirrors the ability of humans to learn, understand, and apply knowledge across unlimited contexts. Discuss with your team: should we want to get there? How will our lives change if and when we do?

links.label: BBC Science Focus - AI bubble and AGI limits

entries.studentExplanation:
Artificial general intelligence, or AGI, would be much broader than today’s task-specific AI. Current systems can write, summarize, code, classify, or generate media impressively, but they still struggle with reliability, grounding, outliers, and true understanding. The Science Focus article is useful because it challenges the assumption that simply scaling bigger models will automatically reach AGI. Students should understand the key debate: the question is not only whether AGI is possible, but whether society should race toward something that could transform work, knowledge, power, and human self-understanding.

Use narrow AI versus AGI as the first distinction. Current systems can be impressive and brittle at once: they generate fluent work, hallucinate, depend on data and evaluation, and struggle with rare cases. The real question is what human institutions would change if a general system became reliable. Use this to audit promises: what future is being predicted, when people expected it, how technical or social limits delayed it, and why “soon” can be a feeling rather than a calendar date.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this helps students distinguish current AI from the larger AGI idea. At the section level, it raises the stakes of futurism by asking what kind of intelligence society is trying to build. At the year-theme level, it connects to bright/dark futures because AGI can be imagined as salvation, disruption, or both.
entries.takeaway: The real destination is not only technical but civilizational
examples: Scaling laws and the AI bubble
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating whether AGI should be treated as a technical destination, an ethical threshold, or a speculative distraction.
entries.counterargument: AGI is speculative enough that both utopian and catastrophic claims can outrun the evidence.

items.title: Narrow AI vs AGI

entries.title: The Next Frontier Underground
entries.rawOfficialText: “Because it’s there,” the British explorer George Mallory once responded, when asked why he wanted to climb Mt. Everest. (It’s not clear if he ever got there.) During the Cold War, both the United States and the Soviet Union looked the opposite direction and launched rival projects to drill as deep as possible into the Earth’s crust. The United States made it about 600 meters; the Soviet Union, about 12,000—resulting in the Kola Superdeep Borehole. The project only shut down in 1992 after the Soviet Union itself went into a hole. Learn more about this project and others like it, then discuss with your team: what, if anything, should be the next frontier humanity tries to reach—and why? And are races—and heated rivalries—the best way to get somewhere quickly?

links.label: BBC Future - the deepest hole ever dug

entries.studentExplanation:
The Kola Superdeep Borehole reminds students that the future is not always above us in space or ahead of us in computers. Sometimes the next frontier is below our feet. During the Cold War, the United States and Soviet Union competed to drill deep into Earth’s crust; the Soviet project reached more than 12 kilometers before heat, technical limits, and political collapse ended the effort. The entry asks whether rivalry helps humanity move faster, or whether it can distort exploration into prestige competition.

The Kola Superdeep Borehole and Everest-style “because it’s there” logic show that frontiers are not only outward into space. Underground exploration runs into heat, pressure, tools, funding, and national rivalry, so “frontier” means a limit where curiosity meets physical resistance. Use this to audit promises: what future is being predicted, when people expected it, how technical or social limits delayed it, and why “soon” can be a feeling rather than a calendar date.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this shows that frontiers are chosen and culturally framed. At the section level, it closes futurism by asking not just what we can chase, but why we chase it. At the year-theme level, it connects to progress and prediction because getting “there” depends on values, not only distance.
entries.takeaway: Whether competition helps discovery or distorts the reasons for exploring
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating whether human frontiers should be chosen through rivalry, shared curiosity, prestige, or public need.
entries.counterargument: Rivalry can distort priorities, but it can also accelerate effort in ways purely cooperative systems may not always match.

items.title: “Because it’s there”
items.note: Mallory’s Everest phrase frames exploration as desire, challenge, and prestige, not only practical need.

The image must be realistic, not anime, and must feel visually compatible with the beige/brown/maroon WSC app UI; WSC app mandatory. items.note: At extreme depth, high temperature and rock behavior made drilling far harder than surface ambition suggested.

items.title: Rivalry vs cooperation
items.note: Races can speed exploration, but they can also make prestige more important than knowledge or safety.
items.imageGuidance: Use /imagesearch first to find a realistic split image of two scientific teams or nations competing to reach a frontier, in WSC app style. The image must be realistic, not anime, and must feel visually compatible with the beige/brown/maroon WSC app UI; WSC app mandatory. If /imagesearch cannot find a perfect image and the slide is illustrating a general idea rather than an exact known source, use /imagecreator or /imagegen to create a realistic educational image that illustrates the concept while respecting the World Scholar app theme.
items.videoGuidance: Use /youtubesearch to find a clear educational video, ideally 5-7 minutes maximum or shorter, that explains how Cold War rivalry shaped science and exploration. Avoid hype clips, product ads, sensational thumbnails, or videos that only decorate the topic.

items.title: Choosing the next frontier
items.note: The final question is philosophical: which frontiers are worth pursuing, and who gets to decide?
items.imageGuidance: Use /imagesearch first to find a realistic WSC-style classroom debate image with students comparing space, ocean, deep earth, AI, and medicine as possible future frontiers. The image must be realistic, not anime, and must feel visually compatible with the beige/brown/maroon WSC app UI; WSC app mandatory. If /imagesearch cannot find a perfect image and the slide is illustrating a general idea rather than an exact known source, use /imagecreator or /imagegen to create a realistic educational image that illustrates the concept while respecting the World Scholar app theme.
items.videoGuidance: Use /youtubesearch to find a clear educational video, ideally 5-7 minutes maximum or shorter, that explains scientific frontiers and why humans explore. Avoid hype clips, product ads, sensational thumbnails, or videos that only decorate the topic.
